• 🏗️ Cryogenic OGS Limited incorporates "Infravolt Engineering Private Limited" on May 4, 2026 — Certificate of Incorporation issued by RoC, Ahmedabad

• ⚙️ Infravolt Engineering to manufacture precision components for power, energy and railway infrastructure applications — also aimed at backward integration in related industry segments

• 📊 Cryogenic OGS holds 51% of paid-up share capital (25,500 equity shares) | Authorised capital: ₹10 Lakh | Paid-up capital: ₹5 Lakh | Shares subscribed at face value of ₹10 per share

• 📋 Earlier intimation of incorporation proposal filed on April 24, 2026 — incorporation completed within 10 days

🟢 Impact: Positive (Strategic subsidiary in engineering sector — backward integration play targeting power, energy & railways; nominal initial capital with long-term diversification potential)

text:
EMS LIMITED : RECEIVES ₹143.80 CR ORDER FROM UP JAL NIGAM FOR SEWER NETWORK IN VARANASI 🚰📋

• 🏆 EMS Limited receives Letter of Award (LOA) from UP Jal Nigam (Urban), Varanasi for laying of Sewer Network and House Connection work in 5 Wards (Durgakund, Nariyan, Sarainandan, Jolha Northan & Bhelupur) out of 18 Problematic Wards of Nagar Nigam, Varanasi

• 💰 Order value: ₹143.80 Cr (approx. ₹14,379.53 lakhs, excluding GST) | Execution timeline: 24 months

• 🔧 Scope: Turnkey basis — surveying, soil investigation, design, material supply, laying & jointing of sewer network and house connections

• 📋 EMS had received L1 (lowest bidder) status for this tender on April 17, 2026 — LOA confirmed within ~17 days

🟢 Impact: Positive (Significant government infrastructure order win — adds to order book & provides 24-month revenue visibility in the urban water/sanitation segment)

text:
RAINBOW CHILDREN’S MEDICARE : EXPANDS BENGALURU NETWORK 🏥👶

• 🏥 New Hospital Launch (HRBR, Bengaluru)
– Commissioned 60-bed pediatric & perinatal hospital
– Strengthens presence in North Bengaluru high-growth corridor

• 🤱 IVF Expansion (Mahadevapura)
– Launched dedicated IVF centre under BirthRight platform
– Extension of Marathahalli hub → strengthens fertility network

• 🌐 Network Expansion
– Total network now ~600 beds across 6 hospitals + clinics & IVF centres in Bengaluru
– Overall company capacity rises to ~2,435 beds

• 📈 Strategic Benefits
– Enhances hub-and-spoke model efficiency & referral network
– Targets young urban population & rising fertility demand
– Strengthens leadership in pediatric + perinatal care

• 💰 Capex & Funding
– Total investment: ~₹52 Cr (Hospital ₹42 Cr + IVF ₹10 Cr)
– Funded via internal accruals (no balance sheet stress)

• ⚙️ Operational
– Facilities operational from May 4, 2026
– Current occupancy ~46.6% (9M FY26) → scope for ramp-up

🟢 Impact: Positive (Capacity expansion in high-growth Bengaluru + asset-light IVF addition supports long-term revenue & margin visibility)

text:
RRKabel:
While most were focused on Polycab & KEI,
RR Kabel delivered an absolute blockbuster set of numbers.

More importantly, they have given a strong guidance of 16-18% volume growth, which can translate to 25-30% revenue growth.
On top of that, margins are set to expand from current 8.1% in FY26 to 10.5% in FY28 due to operating leverage and also change in product mix - all their recent capex is towards HV & EHV cables which have higher margins.
Also, FMEG segment is growing fast and operating losses are reducing, expect FMEG segment to break even at EBITDA level in FY27
SO, PAT growth can be much higher at 50%.
Even FY28 should also be another strong growth year.

While Polycab & Kei are already operating at higher margins, RR Kabel is playing catch up and that's where the big opportunity is there.
As per my estimates, stock is trading at 25PE based on FY27 PAT, which makes it cheapest in the Cables&wires segment.

Disc: no recos, do your own research

W&C stocks showing good momentum today🟩

RR Kabel reported a solid set & is catching up in terms of valuations, as it was trading at a significant discount compared to peers (Polycab & KEI).

Delivered a strong FY26, with the wires & cables segment continuing to drive overall growth.

Management highlighted that demand remained healthy across domestic & export markets, supported by infrastructure, housing, industrial activity & B2B expansion.

Company remains on track with its Project Rise roadmap, which targets 18% CAGR in W&C & 25% CAGR in FMEG, translating into 2.5x cumulative EBITDA growth over 3 years (FY25-28)

W&C contributed 90% of total REV in FY26, and the segment delivered strong growth, supported by higher volumes, better pricing, export momentum & operating leverage.

For FY27, management expects W&C volume growth to remain in the 16-18% range, with cables expected to grow faster than wires.

Cable contribution will increase in the coming quarters, and this mix improvement should support better margins over time.

On margins, Management said the company has already achieved around 130 bps improvement in FY26 under Project Rise.

The target is to improve W&C margins by 300 bps by FY28. For FY27, management has guided for around 9.5% margin, and by FY28, the target is to reach towards double digits.

Margin improvement will be driven by scale benefits, higher cable mix, better product mix, operating efficiencies & export growth.

The 1,200 Cr capex plan for FY26-28 is progressing as planned. Capacity addition will happen gradually every 6 months rather than one large capacity addition.

A key upgrade is that RR Kabel currently manufactures up to 66 KV cables, and after the new capex, it will be able to manufacture up to 220 KV cables.
Data centers are also emerging as an important growth opportunity.

FMEG remained weak in FY26, but losses have reduced.
FY27 FMEG growth is expected to be supported by fans, switches, switchgears, appliances & other categories, and management expects FMEG to achieve breakeven in FY27.

It is already under our coverage from lower levels, not a reco to buy/sell.

NESTLE INDIA: CO’S FOCUS REMAINS ON VOLUME-LED GROWTH, SAYS CMD - BL
MARUTI SUZUKI: CO STARTS FY27 WITH 42% RISE IN MARKET SHARE || CO'S INCREASE IN MARKET SHARE HAS BEEN LED BY ITS PASSENGER CARS, SUVS HAVE ALSO MADE A SIGNIFICANT CONTRIBUTION. - BS

Centre Achieves 98% of FY26 Revised Capex Target at ₹10.75 Lakh Crore; Railways and NHAI Drive 47% of Spending

FY26 Capex Execution
Actual capex spent: ₹10.75 lakh crore — ~98% of revised estimate of ₹10.96 lakh crore

Original budget estimate for FY26: ₹11.21 lakh crore — revised downward to ₹10.96 lakh crore at Budget stage

Government official: "The capex achievement is very good… momentum in asset-creating expenditure continued"

April–February FY26 capex growth: 14.5% YoY (latest official data available)

Who Led the Spending
Railways: deployed ₹2.6 lakh crore

NHAI: deployed ₹2.4 lakh crore

Together: accounted for 47% of total capital expenditure in FY26

Centre also nearly fully utilised ₹1.5 lakh crore earmarked for 50-year interest-free capex loans to states

Capex as % of GDP — Trend
FY22: 2.5% | FY23: 2.8% | FY24: 3.2% | FY25: 3.2% | FY26 RE: 3.1%

Capex has risen sharply from 1.7% of GDP in FY20 — structural step-up sustained

Including capital grants to states: estimated at 3.9% of GDP in FY26 RE

FY25 Context
FY25 actual capex: ₹10.52 lakh crore against revised estimate of ₹10.18 lakh crore and budget estimate of ₹11.11 lakh crore

FY27 Outlook
Budgeted capex: ₹12.2 lakh crore, up 11.5% YoY

Effective capital expenditure (including grants-in-aid to states for asset creation): projected to grow 22.1% to ₹17.1 lakh crore

Revenue expenditure (ex-interest and major subsidies): contracted 6% in FY26 — fiscal consolidation via revenue containment, not capex compression

Fiscal Consolidation Path
Revenue expenditure contained at 10.5% of GDP in FY27, down from 10.8% in FY26 RE

Capital expenditure maintained at 3.1% of GDP — government choosing to protect investment spending

Strategy: compress revenue spend, sustain capex — textbook counter-cyclical fiscal management

Core Theme
The Centre's near-complete execution of its FY26 capex target — with Railways and NHAI alone deploying ₹5 lakh crore — confirms that India's public investment engine remains the primary growth multiplier, even as the government simultaneously consolidates its fiscal position by compressing revenue expenditure rather than cutting capital spending.

CITI on Voda Idea

Buy, High risk – TP Rs 14

Years-long AGR saga for Voda Idea has finally concluded, with gov’t reassessing the company’s AGR dues at Rs640bn as of Dec’25, which is 20% below the Rs805bn that was outstanding as per co

With no interest accruing & effective 10-year repayment moratorium remaining in place (99% of dues are payable over FY36-41), this meaningfully improves economics of liability, reducing VI’s effective AGR burden further from an estimated c.Rs350bn to c.Rs260bn on an NPV basis

With this chapter of regulatory uncertainty now largely behind it, believe VI is better positioned to close its pending c.Rs250bn bank debt raise, which would in turn enable it to commence execution of its Rs450bn three-year capex plan that was outlined by mgm’t in the Jan’26 strategy update

Closure of this debt funding will therefore now be key to monitor.

Why FII & DII Increased Holding Matters
When both Foreign Institutional Investors (FIIs) and Domestic Institutional Investors (DIIs) increase their stake in a company, it reflects strong institutional confidence in the business.

FIIs bring global perspective, capital flows, and macro conviction

DIIs (mutual funds, insurance, etc.) bring local expertise and long-term conviction

When both align:
1. It signals high-quality business + earnings visibility Indicates strong management credibility and growth outlook
2. Often leads to better liquidity and sustained price support
3. Suggests the company is being accumulated by smart money

Such businesses are worth tracking and studying because institutional accumulation generally happens before major price moves, not after.

India's aerospace supply chain is not a defence budget story. It is a supply chain capture story!!

👉The full chain recap:
  🔹Aircraft Owners → IndiGo, GMR Airports, Air India
  🔹OEM → HAL, BDL, L&T, BEL 
  🔹Tier 1 → Dynamatic, BEL, Data Patterns, Mahindra Aero 
  🔹Tier 2 → Azad Engineering, MTAR, Unimech, Midhani 
  🔹Tier 3 → Sansera, Rossell, ideaForge, Zen Technologies 
  🔹Tier 4 → Midhani, Hindalco, Bharat Forge, Kaynes
  🔹MRO → HAL, GMR Airports, Sika Interplant, BEL

👉The cross-chain compounders that appear across multiple tiers: 
  🔹HAL 
  🔹BEL 
  🔹Bharat Forge 
  🔹L&T 
  🔹Dynamatic Technologies 
  🔹MTAR Technologies 
  🔹Midhani

👉India was 2% of global aerospace supply chains 3 years ago. The target is 10%.
👉The contracted evidence - TASL's Apache fuselages, Dynamatic's A220 doors, Azad's Mitsubishi contract - shows the transition is already underway. The market hasn't fully priced it yet.

Disclaimer: Do not consider it as a buy/sell recommendation, just for information!! Do your own due diligence.

Layer: MRO - Maintenance, Repair & Overhaul. The most obvious opportunity that's most under-invested.

👉Here is the staggering reality: 90% of India's aircraft MRO is outsourced to Singapore, Dubai, Sri Lanka, and Malaysia.
👉India spends approximately ₹15,000 crore per year sending its aircraft abroad for maintenance - money that should stay in India.
👉India MRO market: $3.77B (2024) → $6.87B (2033) at 6.4% CAGR.
  🔹HAL: The only full-service engine overhaul capability in India. Defence MRO dominant. Su-30MKI, Tejas, Dhruv, HTT-40.
  🔹GMR Aero Technic (via GMR Airports): The most credible civil MRO play. Hyderabad International Airport facility. Narrow-body + wide-body servicing. Indirect exposure through GMR Airports Infrastructure.
  🔹BEL + Data Patterns: Avionics and electronics MRO for defence platforms - high-value, non-commoditised work.
  🔹Sika Interplant Systems: MRO services for landing gear, avionics, interconnection systems. Small-cap, overlooked, revenues doubling over 4 years.

👉Government Vision 2040: India to meet 90% of its own MRO needs and earn 20% revenue from foreign aircraft. That is a complete reversal of a 90% import dependency. The listed plays positioned for that reversal are underpriced today

Disclaimer: Do not consider it as a buy/sell recommendation, just for information!! Do your own due diligence.

Layer: Tier 4 - Raw Materials & Processes. The foundation everything else is built on!!

👉This is the least glamorous layer. It is also the most strategically critical - because if Tier 4 fails, every layer above it fails.
  🔹Midhani: Titanium, superalloys, special steels. Strategic monopoly. Every Indian fighter aircraft, rocket, and submarine has Midhani materials inside it. Undervalued relative to its strategic importance.
  🔹Hindalco Industries: Aerospace-grade aluminium - the structural material of every civil aircraft. DC + EV + Aerospace all pulling on the same aluminium supply simultaneously.
  🔹Bharat Forge: Forgings and castings for aerospace applications. Global aerospace exposure through its international subsidiaries alongside domestic defence contracts.
  🔹Kaynes Technology: EMS player entering aerospace-grade PCB and electronics manufacturing. Early stage in this segment - but high-margin, high-barrier work if quality certifications achieved.
  🔹Samvardhana Motherson: Wiring harnesses across auto and aerospace. Less pure-play but genuinely exposed.

👉The Tier 4 insight: India still imports significant volumes of aerospace-grade titanium, carbon fibre composites, and specialty alloys. Midhani's Strategic Metal Bank MoU with HAL is the first serious attempt at supply chain sovereignty at this layer

Disclaimer: Do not consider it as a buy/sell recommendation, just for information!! Do your own due diligence.

Layer: Tier 3 - Make-to-Print. High volume. Durable moats. Silent compounding!!

👉Tier 3 = OEM gives you the design, you execute it at precision. Print-to-Part contracts. This is where India's cost advantage (30-40% vs US/EU peers) is the most structurally powerful.
  🔹Sansera Engineering: CNC machining, aerospace components, forging. Order book ₹6,500 Cr+ (Q3 FY26). Management guiding 30% revenue growth FY26, 33-35% long-term EBITDA target.
  🔹Rossell Techsys: Wiring harnesses and interconnection systems for civil and military aircraft. Small-cap, under-covered, direct exposure to fleet expansion tailwind.
  🔹Paras Defence & Space Tech: Aerospace structures and systems. Optics for space and defence. One of the few Indian plays on space + defence electronics combined.
  🔹ideaForge Technology: India's leading domestic drone maker. Indigenous UAV design + manufacturing. Defence and civilian applications.
  🔹Zen Technologies: Combat training simulators + anti-drone systems. Order book crossed ₹1 lakh crore in Jan 2026. Targeting ₹4,000 Cr revenue in 2–3 years with export ambitions in Europe and US.

👉Entry into this tier requires AS9100D certification and NADCAP accreditation - qualification takes 2-5 years. That delay is the moat

Disclaimer: Do not consider it as a buy/sell recommendation, just for information!! Do your own due diligence.

Layer: Tier 2 - Sub-Assembly & Principal Components. The most underdiscovered alpha layer!!

👉Tier 2 = complex sub-assemblies. Not just a machined part - an integrated component that goes directly into a Tier 1 system.
🔹Azad Engineering: The most under-owned quality name in India's aerospace chain. Signed a $156M multi-year contract with Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (Sep 2025) - more than double its annual revenue at signing. Honeywell contract won (Oct 2024). Supplies to GE Aerospace, Safran, Rolls-Royce. 86.5% gross margin - highest in peer group.
  🔹MTAR Technologies: Precision engineering for ISRO, DRDO, GKN Aerospace. 600+ advanced CNC machines, 5–10 micron tolerances. Expanding into fuel cells + AI data center energy systems.
  🔹Unimech Aerospace: 121% revenue growth FY24. 37.9% EBITDA margins. Recently listed. Precision components for global OEMs with AS9100D certification.
  🔹Midhani (Mishra Dhatu Nigam): The strategic materials monopoly. Only Indian manufacturer of aerospace-grade titanium, superalloys, and special steels. HAL signed an MoU with Midhani in 2025 to create a Strategic Metal Bank. This is a national security asset, not just a company.

Disclaimer: Do not consider it as a buy/sell recommendation, just for information!! Do your own due diligence.

Layer: Tier 1 - Systems Integration. Where India is graduating from component maker to partner!!

👉Tier 1 = you supply integrated systems, not just parts. This is where trust, certification, and long-term contracts compound.
  🔹Dynamatic Technologies: Airbus A320/A330 flap track beams. In 2024, won the contract to manufacture ALL door variants for ALL A220 aircraft globally - the largest aerospace export contract ever awarded to an Indian private company. Also making F-15EX Eagle II components for Boeing and Falcon jets for Dassault. Sub ₹1,000 Cr market cap. Wildly under-owned.
  🔹Bharat Electronics (BEL): The avionics backbone of India's entire defence fleet. Radars, sonars, communication, fire control - every Indian military platform has BEL inside it.
  🔹Data Patterns India: Defence electronics, radars, avionics, missile subsystems. 42.6% EBITDA margins. The quality business most people still haven't found.
  🔹Mahindra Aerostructures: $300M Aernnova contract + Airbus H125 fuselage contract. Recognised in top-4 most advanced manufacturing sites globally at Paris Air Show 2025.

Disclaimer: Do not consider it as a buy/sell recommendation, just for information!! Do your own due diligence.

Layer: OEM - India's Prime Manufacturers!!

👉This is the top of the pyramid. The smallest layer by company count, the largest by strategic importance.
  🔹HAL: Tejas Mk1A (83 aircraft, ₹48,000 Cr), AMCA, LCH, HTT-40 - order book exceeds ₹1 lakh crore. In June 2025, ISRO transferred complete SSLV rocket technology to HAL - first time ISRO has ever transferred an entire rocket's technology to a single company.
  🔹Tata Advanced Systems (TASL): India's first private aircraft Final Assembly Line - Airbus C295 at Vadodara. Also operates Tata Boeing Aerospace (delivered its 300th Apache helicopter fuselage, Feb 2025). TASL is not listed - but the Tata Group exposure is via Tata Sons ecosystem.
  🔹BDL: India's missile manufacturer. Surface-to-air, air-to-air, anti-tank. Captive government buyer, long-order-book visibility.
  🔹L&T Defence: Naval systems, missile launchers, AMCA consortium member. Aerospace is a growing sub-segment of a larger engineering giant.

👉The OEM layer is dominated by PSUs. The private sector is entering - but the qualification cycles are 5-10 years. The moat is enormous

Disclaimer: Do not consider it as a buy/sell recommendation, just for information!! Do your own due diligence.

Before the supply chain - the demand picture. Because this is what's pulling every layer simultaneously, India will become the world's 3rd largest aviation market by 2030.!!

  🔹IndiGo: 500 Airbus A320s on order 
  🔹Air India: 470 aircraft (Boeing + Airbus) 
  🔹Akasa: 150 Boeing 737 MAX (2024) Total: 1,200+ aircraft on order in India alone.

👉On the defence side: 
  🔹India's defence budget FY26: ₹6.81 lakh crore - +9.5% YoY 
  🔹₹20,000 crore earmarked for domestically manufactured weapons 
  🔹HAL alone signed a ₹62,370 crore deal (domes) in Sept 2025

👉Every aircraft ordered is MRO demand deferred.  Every defence platform is a decade-long supply chain commitment.
👉The demand is structural, policy-backed, and accelerating

Disclaimer: Do not consider it as a buy/sell recommendation, just for information!! Do your own due diligence.

India accounts for less than 2% of the global aerospace supply chain today!!

👉Analysts expect that number to hit 10% within a decade.

👉That is a 5x expansion in global market share - in one of the most complex,highest-barrier, longest-cycle industries on earth.

👉This is not a defence budget story. This is a supply chain capture story.

👉I've mapped the full aerospace value chain - Aircraft Owners → OEM → Tier 1 → Tier 2 → Tier 3 → Tier 4 → MRO - with every meaningful listed Indian play at every level.

 Let's go layer by layer

text:
Putting It All Together: Where to focus

I conclude with a ranked opportunity map:

Smart Grid + Power Electronics (Zone 1): Genus-type smart-metering firms and inverter/PCS makers. These start small but become indispensable. Expect c.30%+ CAGR as RDSS/meters and grid-scale storage accelerate. High probability compounders here (control layer).

Specialty Chemicals (Zone 2): Neogen/Balaji/Tatva – selective hits. Look for companies with dedicated battery-chemistry capacity. Odds improve if India rolls out manufacturing (PLI) and import cuts.

Asset Owners (Zone 3): Oriana-type. Betting on BOO models is binary: either flat or multi-10x if they secure power-purchase or DBFOOT deals. Capital-intensive and execution-heavy.

EPC Contractors (Zone 4): Bondada/Advait/Pace – great revenue, thin margins. These are mid-cycle plays for GDP-like growth, not long-term compounders. Treat them as satellites.

Credit : Shubham Jain

Each of the above companies plays a niche but critical role in BESS/Smart Grid:

Neogen: Tied up with Mitsubishi (MUIS) to set up a 30ktp year electrolytes plant. Hidden Catalysts include India’s ACC PLI scheme (50 GWh by 2030) and lithium price trends; the main risk is raw material import dependence and capex scaling.

Balaji Amines: Only domestic electronic-grade DMC producer (EV battery grade) after installing a new unit in May 2025. Virtually debt-free. Risk is the cost of ammonia feedstock and slower-than-expected EV battery demand.

Tatva Chintan: Largest in India for electrolyte salts/glymes (supercapacitor batteries). The catalyst is any push toward supercaps or specialty battery chem. Risks: sluggish pharma/chemical cycles, low ROE.

HPL Electric: 20% meter market share. With RDSS smart-meter tenders accelerating, HPL will ramp volumes. Hidden: potential move up the value chain via software. Risk: stretched receivables and global material costs.

RMC Switchgears: Niche supplier of meter boxes and distribution panels. As states roll out 100m+ smart meters, RMC benefits. But high receivables (170d) indicate tight liquidity.

Advait Energy: Core is transmission line products/EPC (conductors, OPGW). Its catalyst is the recent orderbook (₹1,048 Cr) and diversification into electrolysers and BESS EPC. Kedia’s stake highlights retail faith. Risks include high working capital needs and capital dilution for new ventures.

Bondada Engineering: Secured a Build-Own-Operate (BOO) deal from APTRANSCO: 225 MW/450 MWh BESS – a landmark order. Catalyst: success in BOO could open more deals. Risk: Just one project, execution complexity, political/sector risk.

Pace Digitek: While known as a telco infra player, it now manufactures & installs telecom batteries and Li units. It’s quietly building telecom and data-center power projects. Catalyst: telecom capex cycle revival, 5G rollout. Risk: high debtors (276d) and reliance on select large contracts.

Swelect Energy: Beyond 1 GW solar panels, its key move is launching bi-directional inverters – essential for V2G and grid storage. Also builds PV inverters and BESS container solutions. Catalyst: local inverter content push. Risk: low margins and competition (SolarEdge, Growatt).

Exicom Tele-Systems: Veteran maker of DC rectifiers and EV chargers. The new 10 MW Hyderabad EV charger unit (FY26 capex) will boost capacity. It serves telecom and EV. Catalyst: India’s EV charging build-out. Risk: legacy telecom slowdown, losses in recent quarters (negative EBITDA).

Each of these stocks trades at relatively modest multiples today. Their common bet is on India’s policy tailwinds (PLI, smart grid schemes, renewable push) and structural shortfall in domestic supply chains. The risks we note – liquidity, execution, debt – are real and warrant caution. Our thesis is that surviving and scaling in these niches will re-rate only a few of them strongly.
I have shared 11 small-cap stocks across the four niches.

Beyond Cells: The ₹ per 1 GWh Value Stack

BESS is a system, not just batteries. A typical 1 GWh/1 GW (10-hour) utility-scale project in India (~₹4,000–5,000 Cr) includes:

Cells & Packs: ~40–50% of cost (₹1,800–2,500 Cr) – largest single slice, but commodity-like margins.

Power Electronics (PCS/Inverters): ~15–20% (₹700–1,000 Cr) – bi-directional converters, DC-AC inverters.

Balance of System (BOS): ~10–15% (transformers, cabling, wiring, etc.).

Engineering/Integration (EPC): ~10–15% (design, construction) – often part of the above share.

Software & EMS: ~5–10% (Energy Management Systems, SCADA).

O&M (lifecycle): ~5–10% of total over 20 yrs (maintenance, services).

On revenue share, cells dominate. But on profit share, the value flips. Cells (often imported) are competitive with thin margins. EPC is high revenue but low margin (10–15% at best) and heavy working capital. The real fat profits lie in the control layer: PCS/inverters (especially in a nascent market needing indigenization) and smart grid/EMS (low competition, recurring models).

For example, Swelect and Exicom supply high-end inverters and chargers, capturing ~15–20% of the system cost, yet they command healthy EBITDA rates. Similarly, Genus Power (smart meters) earns recurring fee-based revenues. In sum:

Cells → scale wins (but margin-squeeze).
EPC → volume wins (growth, but returns low).
Electronics/Controls → tech wins (high-margin niche).
Software/EMS/AMI → platform wins (annuity growth).

This framework is central: the “brains” of BESS will create the richest companies, not the “brawn” (raw batteries).

India’s Peak: 256 GW vs 0.1% Storage

On 25 April 2026 at 3:38 PM, India’s all-time peak demand hit 256.117 MW, crushing the previous 250 GW record (May 2024). Crucially, no shortage or grid stress was reported. The difference? An enormous generation base (65 GW added in FY26) and a diverse fuel mix. Solar output was “especially critical” during the peak, but conventional (thermal, hydro, nuclear) and inter-state coordination also played key roles.

However, the footnote is stark: BESS (and PSP) contributed just 201 MW – 0.1% of generation. In other words, the grid kept running despite almost no utility-scale storage. As industry observers note, that 0.1% “gap” will not persist. Once storage is affordable and scaled, grid operations transform.

Indeed, BESS costs have collapsed by ~80% in recent years, largely due to technology maturation and policy (e.g., PLI schemes). The Ministry of Power now confirms BESS tariff bids as low as ₹2.1/kWh (assuming 2 cycles/day), down from ₹10.18/kWh in 2022–23. Even at 1.5 cycles/day, it’s ₹2.8/kWh, comparable to solar’s ₹2.5–3/kWh. This drastic cost drop, coupled with viability-gap funding (VGF), interstate charge waivers, and ambitious capacity targets, means grid-scale storage is now viable and imminent.

Implication: Demand growth is no longer the bottleneck – execution is. India can now afford to deploy hundreds of GWh of storage. The question shifts to who builds and benefits from this ecosystem.

Beyond Batteries: Mapping India’s ₹5,000 Cr BESS Value Chain

From EPC builders to smart grid enablers, this deep dive breaks down where value actually accrues—and which small-cap players are positioned to benefit as India’s storage ecosystem scales.

On 25 April 2026, India’s grid set a new record – 256.1 GW peak demand – and yet battery storage (PSP+BESS) accounted for only 201 MW (0.1%). This gulf between demand and storage capacity signals a tectonic shift ahead. In just a few years, BESS costs have plunged (~₹10.2→₹2.1/unit), policy incentives are accelerating rollout (PLI, VGF, transmission waivers), and the 2026 peak hints that storing & controlling power is the next frontier.

But investors often fixate on battery cells alone. In reality, each 1 GWh BESS (~₹4–5k Cr) is a complex system. Our deep research shows that ~45% of the value lies outside the cell (power electronics, integration, software, etc.). The future winners won’t just be battery makers – they’ll be the system enablers and controllers of energy flow.

This article weaves a narrative through: India’s April 2026 peak and policy landscape, the BESS value-chain breakdown (₹/kWh), a three-phase evolution of the market, and a “₹100→₹1000 Cr” multibagger opportunity map. I have identified 10 small-cap stocks across four niches (Battery components, Smart Grid/AMI, BESS EPC, Power Electronics), highlighting their products, hidden catalysts, and risks. Finally, I rank sectors by wealth-creation potential with evidence-based reasoning throughout.

256 GW demand.
0.1% storage.
That gap will reshape India’s power sector.

text:
Avenue Supermarts (DMart) - Q4 FY26 Result Analysis

Q4 Revenue: ₹17,683.86 Cr. Up 18.9% YoY. 
Q4 EBITDA: ₹1,210.53 Cr. Up 26.8% YoY. 
Q4 EBITDA margin: 6.85%. Up 42 bps YoY. 
Q4 PAT to owners: ₹656.59 Cr. Up 19.2% YoY.

Q4 showed operating leverage. EBITDA grew faster than revenue.

EBITDA +26.8% YoY on revenue +18.9%, that 800 bps gap is operating leverage showing up. Purchases grew in line with revenue (+18.9%). Other expenses grew 12.0% YoY, well below revenue. The margin expanded 42 bps YoY to 6.85% in Q4.

For the full year FY26: EBITDA ₹5,187 Cr on revenue ₹68,821 Cr,  7.54% margin, down just 2 bps YoY. Management had guided they would accept a minor margin dip for faster growth. 2 bps is essentially flat. They delivered on that commitment.

85 stores in FY26. Guidance was >50.

DMart added 85 new stores in FY26, taking the total to 500, a milestone. The guidance floor was "not less than 50." They added 70% more than the minimum. Store growth rate: 20.5%, at the upper end of the guided 10–20% range. Revenue grew 15.9% YoY, ahead of store count growth, meaning existing stores are still contributing positively.

The two structural cost headwinds: employee costs and DMart Ready.

Employee costs FY26: ₹1,541.48 Cr, up 32.2% YoY. Revenue grew 15.9%. Employee costs grew at exactly 2x the rate of revenue. Wage inflation for entry-level positions compounded by rapid store expansion headcount. No sign of quick moderation.

Finance costs FY26: ₹142.05 Cr, up 104.5% YoY. 85% of this is lease interest (₹120.76 Cr) under IndAS 116. The owned-store model requires massive upfront capex, ₹4,113.35 Cr in FY26, up 20.2% YoY.

DMart Ready is burning ₹253 Cr of PAT annually.

Subsidiaries (primarily DMart Ready) contributed ₹1,852.71 Cr to consolidated revenue but dragged consolidated PAT by ₹253.44 Cr versus standalone. Standalone PAT FY26: ₹3,223.93 Cr. Consolidated PAT to owners: ₹2,970.49 Cr. Management's timeline is breakeven "within a couple of years." At ₹253 Cr annual burn, that is a meaningful number to watch.

Cash flow: strong operations, structurally negative FCF.

CFO FY26: ₹3,466.74 Cr, up 40.8% YoY. CFO/PAT: 1.17x, high quality. The improvement was driven by trade payables growth as DMart scales its supplier relationships. 

Capex: ₹4,113.35 Cr, FCF: -₹646.61 Cr. The owned-property model generates negative FCF in the expansion phase by design. Despite new commercial paper issuances of ₹965 Cr, D/E stands at just 0.10x, leverage is negligible.

Reported PAT FY26 grew 9.7% YoY. Adjusted for a ₹36.01 Cr one-time tax credit in FY25, normalised growth was 11.2%.

Note : This is not an investment advise.

NETWEB TECH Q4 : MTM losses on payables were offset by hedging gains of ₹5.24 Cr , which are reported under other income. 

For clarity, these gains were added back to EBITDA to reflect true operating performance.

- Cyclical factors played role

- Not purely volume-driven growth  
- External factors influencing data

- Import refunds declined YoY  
- Distorting net collection trends  
- Impacts true demand assessment  

Context & Caveats

- April seasonality inflates numbers

- Requires cautious interpretation

- Strong tax administration impact  
- Digital compliance improving  
- Wider tax base emerging  
- Underlying resilience present  

Core Theme
- Strong headline, weak domestic trend

- Cyclical boost masking reality  
- Structural improvement still ongoing

- Domestic demand needs monitoring  
- Data requires deeper interpretation

APOLLO HOSPITALS – AI & PREVENTIVE HEALTHCARE VIEW

Core Problem
- Diseases appearing earlier in population  
- 1 in 5 under 30 prediabetic  
- Fatty liver often undetected in tests  
- Diseases progress silently for years  

Why Prevention Failed
- System focused on treatment not prevention  
- Insurance and funding favor acute care  
- Prevention lacked economic incentive  
- Symptoms-based detection too late  

What Is Changing
- Employers prioritizing workforce health  
- High obesity and diabetes prevalence  
- Productivity and cost concerns rising  
- Prevention becoming business necessity  

Execution Gap
- Risk detection not leading to outcomes  
- Lack of continuous engagement  
- Follow-up and accountability missing  
- One-time diagnosis insufficient  

Digital Infrastructure
- Integrated EMR, HIS, PACS systems  
- 7.99 million digital consultations FY25  
- 8.5 million lab bookings enabled  
- Strong connected healthcare ecosystem  

Access Expansion
- Teleconsultation bridging city gaps  
- Specialists accessible in smaller towns  
- Seamless data sharing enabled  
- Personalized care becoming scalable  

Role of AI
- Early risk detection before symptoms  
- Continuous monitoring vs one-time tests  
- AI identifies patterns and signals  
- Doctors drive final clinical decisions  

Key Insight
- Data alone has limited value  
- Clinical oversight remains critical  
- AI plus doctors creates real impact  
- Scalable preventive care now possible  

Core Theme
- Shift from reactive to preventive care  
- AI enabling predictive healthcare model  
- Continuous monitoring over episodic care  
- National-scale preventive system emerging  

Final Takeaway
- Preventive healthcare inflection point  
- AI and digital driving transformation  
- Long-term structural healthcare shift

- Not a buy/sell recommendation

INDIA POWER GRID STRESS – PRE-SUMMER ALERT

Demand Surge
- Peak demand hit 256 GW in April  
- Rise of over 40 GW in month  
- Still before peak summer demand  
- Demand trajectory sharply rising  

Supply Deficit
- Evening deficit 3.5–3.9 GW  
- Night shortfall up to 5.4 GW  
- Stress visible before peak season  
- Supply unable to match peaks  

Solar Paradox
- Solar generation up 27% YoY  
- Day prices near ₹1.5/unit  
- Night prices near ₹10/unit  
- Sharp day-night supply mismatch  

Structural Issue
- Daytime surplus, evening shortage  
- Solar drop after sunset critical  
- Demand peaks post-sunset hours  
- Grid imbalance widening structurally  

Generation Mix Stress
- 22 GW thermal under maintenance  
- Gas plants underutilized, costly LNG  
- Renewables curtailment in Rajasthan  
- Coal plants running flexibly  

Infrastructure Pressure
- Transformers degrading 2–3x faster  
- Cooling cycles disrupted at night  
- Grid stress becoming structural  
- Maintenance challenges increasing  

Storage Gap
- Only ~201 MW storage contribution  
- BESS and pumped storage negligible  
- Huge gap between need and capacity  
- Storage critical missing link  

Expert View
- Structural mismatch intensifying  
- Solar shifts stress to evenings  
- Thermal acting balancing resource  
- Storage seen as key solution  

Core Theme
- Solar success creating grid imbalance  
- Cheap power day, costly at night  
- Storage deficit now critical issue  
- Peak summer risk still ahead  

Final Takeaway
- Grid stress rising structurally  
- Urgent need for storage solutions  
- Key risk for power ecosystem  

Disclaimer
- Not a buy/sell recommendation

- Improves product mix and realization  

Order Book Strength
- Order book ₹172 Cr to ₹578 Cr  
- 3.4x growth within one year  
- Strong revenue visibility 24–30 months  
- Healthy pipeline supports growth  

Revenue Growth Path
- TTM revenue around ₹171 Cr  
- FY27 target around ₹300 Cr  
- FY28 target ₹400–500 Cr  
- Strong multi-year growth trajectory  

Valuation View
- FY26E revenue ₹185 Cr  
- FY27E revenue ₹300 Cr  
- FY28E revenue ₹450 Cr  
- Potential re-rating with execution  

Management Guidance
- Peak revenue potential ₹550 Cr  
- FY26 expected to cross ₹200 Cr  
- Capacity utilization key driver  
- Execution critical for targets  

Risk Factors
- Execution delays in new capacity  
- Order inflow sustainability risk  
- Margin pressure from raw materials  
- Dependence on power capex cycle  

Final Takeaway
- Strong play on T&D capex cycle  
- Capacity and EHV shift key triggers  
- High growth with execution dependency

text:
Park Medi World Limited is a rapidly expanding hospital chain focused on delivering affordable, quality healthcare through a highly capital-efficient model. It leverages a cluster-based strategy, brownfield acquisitions, and asset-light O&M arrangements to scale capacity at significantly lower capex per bed than peers, while maintaining clinical standards. The company is also transitioning towards higher-acuity specialties , supported by investments in advanced technologies like robotic surgery, leading to improved realisations and operational efficiency.

A large share of revenue from government healthcare schemes ensures stable patient volumes, while a gradual shift towards private and insurance payors is improving cash flows and reducing working capital intensity. With planned capacity expansion, improving case mix, and benefits from CGHS rate revisions, the company is positioned for steady growth.

text:
⚡ Is Module Overcapacity a Real Concern in India? A Deeper Look  

👇At first glance, rising module capacity may suggest oversupply risk. 

But the reality is more nuanced.

🔹 Supply vs Demand Reality
With ALMM 2 in place, India has ~30 GW of integrated cell + module capacity, while demand is already 50–55 GW.

🔹  Future ALMM 3 (2028) will require backward integration into ingot & wafer, further tightening “usable” supply.

👉 Indicates no industry-wide glut, but selective competitiveness.

🔹 Margins Driven by Integration, Not Capacity
 As cell manufacturing ramps up, players achieve better cost control → margin expansion ahead.

📊 Conclusion:
India’s solar market is not facing overcapacity—
It is transitioning to a high-efficiency, integrated manufacturing ecosystem.

The real differentiator going forward:  Backward integration + execution + product mix.

NUVAMA ON VEDAΝΤΑ

Maintain 'Buy' with an increased target price of ₹936 vs ₹899

Demerger effective May 1, 2026; tailwind of commodity prices

All listed entities are likely to record 19-42% EBITDA CAGR over FY26-28E, except oil & gas

The company's growth projects in aluminium, including bauxite and coal mines, though delayed are likely to be completed in FY27E

Expects the aluminium company to provide DPS of ₹15/share while VEDL can provide ₹5/share in FY27E/28E

EMKAY ON BAJAJ FINANCE

Credit cost guidance revised to ~1.45-1.60% from ~1.65-1.75% due to presentation change

Gold loans, tractor finance, and MFI are likely to grow faster than the portfolio

Company maintains a healthy PCR and has increased coverage on standard assets

Management guides FY27 AUM growth at ~22-24%

Raise EPS ~ 4-8% factoring Q4 FY26 performance

Demand risk from fuel and vehicle price hikes and inflation remain key risks, especially for small cars

Company may find it tough to gain market share, expect SUVs and EVs will keep gaining share in the medium term

Given increased commodity pressure, have lowered margin estimates by ~100 bp, leading to EPS cuts of 10-13% over FY27-28F

Estimate FY27F/28F volumes at 2.67mn, +10% y-y / 2.78mn, +4% y (factoring in FY27F/28F domestic growth of 11%/3%)

NOMURA ON DR REDDY'S LABS

Maintain Buy with an unchanged target price of ₹1,600

Canada's decision augurs well for approval in other markets

Positive impact on financials - primarily due to the potential for high price realization in the early stage of genericization

From a medium-to-long-term perspective, believe the upside will be on account of a higher market share that the company can establish by virtue of being an early entrant

Expect a starting price of ~$60-90 per pen, which may eventually settle at $20 per pen

For 12mn pens, this could imply an initial market size of ~700 mn-1.0 bn which would eventually settle at ~USD250mn

Estimate company can generate revenues of ~$75mn assuming a 30% sustainable market share

NOMURA ON DR REDDY'S LABS

Biosimilar Abatacept is the next key product opportunity to watch out for

In Europe, believe an approval and launch of both the formulations is possible in mid 2027F

Haven't factored any upside from Abatacept in estimates currently because of uncertainty on the regulatory approval

Think an approval and launch in the US and the EU will have a significant and sustained upside for Dr Reddy's as it may remain the only biosimilar in the market for a long period of time

Abatacept can become a $450mn+ annual opportunity for the company

Think sustained growth in non-US markets, could drive a re-rating in the stock

MOTILAL OSWAL ON CEAT

Maintain Buy with target price of ₹4,228 vs ₹3,943

Spurt in costs to test the industry's pricing discipline

PAT beat estimates on higher other income

GST rate cut has helped boost tyre demand, both in replacement and OEM segments

However, the recent surge in input costs is likely to drive near-term margin pressure, as the industry will need a couple of quarters to pass on the entire impact

Remain positive on the long-term benefits that this acquisition can deliver for the group

**SHYAM METALICS – GOLDMAN SACHS VIEW**

• Shyam Metalics and Energy Limited rated Buy; TP ₹1,065 (unchanged)
• Announces incremental capex of ₹27 Bn
• Total capex pipeline now above ₹100 Bn
• Focus on specialty steel, richer product mix
• Positive on volume-led growth outlook
• Valuation ~7x FY28E EV/EBITDA

Impact: Positive

• Discretionary spending recovery expected in H2CY26  
• AI reshaping pricing and productivity models  
• ~40% development cycle now AI-assisted  
• Clients demand integrated human and digital delivery  
• Project Leap launched for operating model transformation  
• Project Leap to deliver $200–300 mn savings  
• Transformation costs at $230–320 mn in CY26  
• Cognizant acquires Astreya for ~$600 mn  
• IT sector witnessing increased M&A activity
